Decentralized Crypto Safety & Autonomous
Trading Platform – Research Summary
1. Competitor Analysis
Crypto Safety Scoring: Existing tools provide vulnerability and risk ratings for tokens/projects. For
example, CertiK offers “Skynet Score,” a real-time security rating for 17,000+ projects . DeFi
Safety (defisafety.com) “evaluates 260+ protocols, 20+ chains, and 25,400+ contracts” based on
development practices . RugDoc IO is a community-driven audit service offering free project
reviews . Other notable services include on-chain monitors like PeckShield and audit firms (e.g.
Quantstamp) that score smart contract quality. These platforms focus on technical audits and on-
chain metrics to flag high-risk tokens.
Social Sentiment Analysis: A number of analytics platforms track crypto social media to gauge
market mood. Santiment aggregates on-chain data and social metrics, claiming to be “#1 in
sentiment crypto metrics” since 2014 . LunarCrush is a popular tool that “filters out the noise in

market mood. Santiment aggregates on-chain data and social metrics, claiming to be “#1 in
sentiment crypto metrics” since 2014 . LunarCrush is a popular tool that “filters out the noise in
the cryptocurrency world” by continuously tracking and summarizing social activity across Twitter ,
Reddit, etc . Other competitors include TheTIE , CoinTrendz , and Valis , which provide sentiment
indices and news impact feeds. Many of these are proprietary, centralized analytics services.
Autonomous Trading Agents: Emerging decentralized platforms let users deploy automated
trading “bots” or AI agents. For example, AIQuant.fun enables users to build AI-driven trading
agents that operate 24/7 with configurable strategies . It supports multi-chain execution and uses
an AI core for decision-making. The RoboFi platform offers a “marketplace of DAO crypto trading bots”
via an Initial Bot Offering (IBO) model , letting users stake or invest in others’ algorithms. Virtuals

via an Initial Bot Offering (IBO) model , letting users stake or invest in others’ algorithms. Virtuals
Protocol bills itself as a “Society of AI Agents – The Wall Street for AI Agents” , providing a
framework (GAME) to co-own and trade AI-driven agents. These platforms emphasize transparency
and shared governance of algorithms.
Figure: Example interface of an AI-powered crypto trading platform. For instance, AIQuant.fun’s web dashboard
lets users create and deploy autonomous, AI-driven trading agents .
2. Profitability & Scalability of Tiered SaaS + Brokerage Model
Tiered SaaS Revenue: A tiered subscription model can generate reliable recurring income. By
offering Free/Basic (limited features), Pro, and Enterprise packages, the platform can serve a wide
range of users (retail traders to institutions). Higher tiers can unlock advanced analytics, higher API
limits, or premium sentiment feeds. SaaS typically enjoys high gross margins (often 70–90%) once

limits, or premium sentiment feeds. SaaS typically enjoys high gross margins (often 70–90%) once
the platform is built, since digital delivery has low incremental cost. The tiered structure also aids
scalability: as more customers join, revenue grows with usage without a proportional rise in costs.
(Industry sources note that tiered pricing aligns revenue with customer value .) •

Brokerage Services: In addition to subscriptions, acting as a crypto broker/aggregator (handling
trades or fiat on-ramps) can add fee-based income. For example, partnering with liquidity providers
or exchanges to execute trades on behalf of users earns commission or spread revenue. This model
scales with trading volume: high-frequency or institutional traders generate more fees. However ,
broker operations require compliance and capital for exchange reserves. Profitability hinges on
capturing sufficient volume and maintaining tight spreads; but it complements SaaS fees by
monetizing market activity directly.
Scalability Considerations: Both SaaS and brokerage models scale well in crypto. Digital tools easily
serve global users and can be deployed across multiple chains. Costs like server infrastructure and
data costs grow sub-linearly with user count. Key challenges are user acquisition and retention

serve global users and can be deployed across multiple chains. Costs like server infrastructure and
data costs grow sub-linearly with user count. Key challenges are user acquisition and retention
(managing churn), and regulatory compliance for fiat/crypto brokerage. Overall, a mixed model
combines stable subscription income with variable but high-potential trading revenues, improving
long-term profitability and scalability.
3. Global Crypto Adoption Trends (Outside India)
Leading Countries (2024): Recent data show rapid growth outside India, especially in developing
regions. Chainalysis reports Nigeria, Indonesia, the USA, Vietnam, etc. as top adopters by index score
. For instance, Nigeria ranks #2 globally (sub-Saharan Africa), and Indonesia and Vietnam are
#3 and #5, respectively . North America and Western Europe also saw surges after events like the
US Bitcoin ETF launch . A TRM Labs report similarly notes that “India, Indonesia, Nigeria, the

#3 and #5, respectively . North America and Western Europe also saw surges after events like the
US Bitcoin ETF launch . A TRM Labs report similarly notes that “India, Indonesia, Nigeria, the
United States, and Vietnam led in crypto adoption in 2024” . Notably, Africa and Latin America
are growing fast (e.g. Kenya doubled its crypto volume year-over-year ).
Country Global Index Rank (2024) Region
India 1 Central & S. Asia/Oceania
Nigeria 2 Sub-Saharan Africa
Indonesia 3 Central & S. Asia/Oceania
United States 4 North America
Vietnam 5 Central & S. Asia/Oceania
Table: Top 5 countries in Chainalysis 2024 Global Crypto Adoption Index (emphasizing outside-India usage).
Usage Metrics: Global crypto ownership is rising. TripleA estimates ~6.8% of the world’s population
(~560 million people) held cryptocurrency in 2024 . Adoption tends to be highest where crypto
solves local problems (remittances, inflation hedge, limited banking). For example, the UAE (24.4%)

(~560 million people) held cryptocurrency in 2024 . Adoption tends to be highest where crypto
solves local problems (remittances, inflation hedge, limited banking). For example, the UAE (24.4%)
and Nigeria (18.9%) have very high ownership rates , reflecting diverse use cases. Overall,
crypto usage is expanding fastest in lower/middle-income regions, but also seeing renewed growth
in developed markets due to institutional products . •

4. Technical Best Practices
Social Sentiment Pipeline: Build a streaming data pipeline ingesting crypto-related feeds (Twitter ,
Reddit, Telegram, news APIs). Use technologies like Apache Kafka or AWS Kinesis for ingestion, then
process text with NLP libraries (e.g. NLTK, HuggingFace Transformers). For example, a live sentiment
analyzer can use Kafka to feed headlines into a Python sentiment model, outputting buy/hold/sell
signals in real time . Design it modularly: one component for data collection, another running
sentiment analysis (potentially as a UDF or sidecar), and a sink to a database or realtime dashboard.
Pre-process text to remove spam/bots and use finance-tuned models (e.g. FinBERT) to improve
accuracy. Incorporate multi-language support for global coverage. Ensure streaming ops are
efficient; as noted by Zhao (2025), traditional pipelines can become “microservices sandwiches”
unless integrated carefully .

efficient; as noted by Zhao (2025), traditional pipelines can become “microservices sandwiches”
unless integrated carefully .
On-Chain Analytics: Adopt a scalable multi-chain data stack . Connect full nodes (or reliable
providers) for each target blockchain and use WebSocket/event streaming to capture blocks and
transactions . Process data with a combination of real-time streaming (e.g. Flink/Kafka
streams) and batch indexing (e.g. The Graph subgraphs) . Index smart-contract events (transfers,
swaps) in a time-series or graph database (e.g. InfluxDB, ClickHouse, Neo4j) to support fast queries
. Provide a GraphQL/REST API layer for analytics clients. Crucially, invest in data quality :
implement robust validation and error handling from day one, since “on-chain analytics is only as
good as the data quality” . Use data integrity checks and reconciliation (e.g. cross-check totals
against on-chain snapshots).

good as the data quality” . Use data integrity checks and reconciliation (e.g. cross-check totals
against on-chain snapshots).
Privacy & Analytics Ethics: When linking off-chain (social) and on-chain data, follow privacy-first
practices. Treat wallet addresses as anonymous identifiers and avoid collecting personal data .
Open-source your tracking SDKs and be transparent about what is captured. Use event-based
analytics (web3 API calls, on-chain events) and respect user opt-outs. Such design builds trust and
complies with emerging Web3 analytics standards .
Autonomous Agent Infrastructure: Architect trading agents using modular , secure components. A
common pattern is a supervisor-collaborator model: a master agent orchestrates sub-agents (e.g. one
for market data, one for trade execution) . Leverage frameworks like LangChain or custom
microservices for this. Agents must securely integrate with blockchain nodes: use reliable providers

for market data, one for trade execution) . Leverage frameworks like LangChain or custom
microservices for this. Agents must securely integrate with blockchain nodes: use reliable providers
(Alchemy, Infura, or BSC/Polygon endpoints) for on-chain data and transactions . Automate
execution via smart-contract schedulers (Chainlink Keepers or Gelato) to trigger actions under
defined conditions. Key management is critical: store any trading wallets’ private keys in hardware
or cloud HSM/KMS solutions (e.g. AWS KMS) to prevent theft . Incorporate real-time data feeds
(prices, mempool, sentiment) and guardrails (e.g. sanity checks on prices) to prevent agent errors.
Designing clear on-chain contracts for agent governance and payments (staking or subscription) will
ensure decentralized execution. •

5. 3-Month Roadmap (Dec 2025 – Feb 2026) Month Learning & Planning Development Milestones Pilot Deployment Dec 2025• Study chosen tech stacks (Kafka, Flink, GraphQL, AI frameworks) and legal requirements.
• Hire/ key team formation (devops, blockchain dev, data scientists).• Define architecture (modular data pipelines, smart-contract design).
• Build initial prototypes: set up blockchain node endpoints, dummy sentiment stream, and a simple trading-agent contract.• N/A (initial proof-of-concept only). Jan 2026• Team training on tools (The Graph, AI model APIs, Chainlink/Gelato).
• Refine regulatory compliance (KYC/AML plan).• Develop core modules: sentiment ingestion/analysis pipeline; on-chain analytics dashboard; basic autonomous-trader prototype (off-chain bot + on-chain interaction).
• Integrate SaaS infrastructure (user auth, tier handling).• Begin internal testing: simulate data flows, run backtests of trading agent on historical data. Feb

interaction).
• Integrate SaaS infrastructure (user auth, tier handling).• Begin internal testing: simulate data flows, run backtests of trading agent on historical data. Feb 2026• Incorporate feedback from tests; documentation and handover notes.• Finalize integration of sentiment, analytics, and trading modules.
• Optimize performance (database indexing, parallel agents).• Launch pilot with select users : provide platform access to a closed group. Gather user feedback and tweak features.
• Demonstrate end-to-end trading cycle (sentiment signal → agent action) on testnet/mainnet. Throughout this period, the team should follow agile sprints. Early focus is on foundational learnings and architecture, shifting to implementation in January, and culminating in an integrated pilot by February. Regular reviews will ensure milestones are met and the platform is secure and functional for launch phases. Sources: Industry reports and platform documentation .

1ACADEMIC INTEGRITY POLICY
1.1Definition of Academic Integrity
Academic integrity is the commitment to honest and responsible scholarship. All members of
theGreenfieldUniversitycommunityareexpectedtoupholdthehigheststandardsofacademic
honesty in all coursework, examinations, research, and scholarly activities.
1.2Forms of Academic Dishonesty
The following are considered violations of academic integrity:
Plagiarism: Presenting someone else’s ideas, words, or work as your own without proper at-
tribution. This includes copying from books, articles, websites, other students’ work, or AI-
generated content without citation.
Cheating: Using unauthorized materials, information, or aids during an examination or as-
signment. This includes looking at another student’s test, using hidden notes, or accessing
prohibited electronic devices.
Fabrication: Falsifying or inventing any information, data, or citation in academic work. This

prohibited electronic devices.
Fabrication: Falsifying or inventing any information, data, or citation in academic work. This
includes making up sources, altering research results, or falsifying laboratory data.
Facilitation: Helping another student commit an act of academic dishonesty, such as allowing
someone to copy your work or taking an exam for another person.
Multiple Submission: Submitting the same work for credit in more than one course without
prior approval from all instructors involved.
1.3Penalties for Academic Integrity Violations
•First Offense: Written warning and mandatory completion of Academic Integrity Work-
shop. The assignment in question will receive a grade of zero.
•Second Offense: Automatic failure of the course (grade of F) and notation on academic
record. Student must petition for removal after one year of good standing.
•ThirdOffense: Suspensionfromtheuniversityforaminimumofoneacademicyear. Read-

record. Student must petition for removal after one year of good standing.
•ThirdOffense: Suspensionfromtheuniversityforaminimumofoneacademicyear. Read-
mission requires formal petition to the Academic Standards Committee.
•Fourth Offense: Permanent expulsion from Greenfield University with notation on per-
manent academic record.
2GRADING POLICIES
2.1Grade Scale
Greenfield University uses the following grade scale:
•A+ (4.0): 97-100% - Exceptional performance
•A (4.0):93-96% - Excellent performance
•A- (3.7): 90-92% - Very good performance
•B+ (3.3): 87-89% - Good performance
•B (3.0):83-86% - Above average performance
•B- (2.7): 80-82% - Satisfactory performance
•C+ (2.3): 77-79% - Average performance
•C (2.0):73-76% - Below average performance
3

•C- (1.7): 70-72% - Minimum passing for major courses
•D+ (1.3): 67-69% - Poor performance
•D (1.0):63-66% - Minimum passing grade
•D- (0.7): 60-62% - Marginal performance
•F (0.0):Below 60% - Failing grade
2.2Grade Point Average (GPA) Requirements
•Good Academic Standing: Cumulative GPA of 2.0 or higher
•Dean’s List: Semester GPA of 3.5 or higher with at least 12 credit hours
•Academic Probation: Cumulative GPA below 2.0
•Academic Dismissal: Three consecutive semesters on probation or GPA below 1.5
2.3Incomplete Grades
AnIncomplete(I)grademaybeassignedwhenastudentisunabletocompletecourserequire-
ments due to circumstances beyond their control (illness, family emergency, etc.). Require-
ments:
•Student must be passing the course at the time of the request
•At least 75% of coursework must be completed
•Written agreement between student and instructor required
•Work must be completed within one semester or grade converts to F
2.4Grade Appeals

•At least 75% of coursework must be completed
•Written agreement between student and instructor required
•Work must be completed within one semester or grade converts to F
2.4Grade Appeals
Students may appeal a final grade within 30 days of grade posting. The appeal process is:
1.Informal discussion with the instructor
2.Written appeal to the Department Chair
3.Formal appeal to the Academic Appeals Committee
4.Final appeal to the Provost (decision is binding)
3ATTENDANCE POLICIES
3.1Class Attendance
Regularattendanceisexpectedinallcourses. Studentsareresponsibleforallmaterialcovered
in class, regardless of attendance.
3.2Excused Absences
The following are considered excused absences with proper documentation:
•Illness requiring medical attention (doctor’s note required)
•Death in immediate family (up to 5 days)
•University-sponsored activities (athletics, debate, etc.)
•Religious observances (advance notice required)
•Jury duty or court appearance (official documentation required)

•University-sponsored activities (athletics, debate, etc.)
•Religious observances (advance notice required)
•Jury duty or court appearance (official documentation required)
•Military service obligations (official orders required)
4

3.3Attendance Impact on Grades
Individual instructors set their own attendance policies, which must be clearly stated in the
course syllabus. However, excessive absences (missing more than 20% of class sessions) may
result in automatic course failure at the instructor’s discretion.
3.4Tardiness Policy
Arriving more than 10 minutes late to class may be counted as an absence. Three instances of
tardiness equal one absence.
4REGISTRATION AND ENROLLMENT
4.1Course Registration
Students must register for courses during the designated registration period. Priority registra-
tion is available for:
•Seniors (90+ credit hours)
•Juniors (60-89 credit hours)
•Sophomores (30-59 credit hours)
•Freshmen (0-29 credit hours)
4.2Credit Hour Limits
•Minimum full-time enrollment: 12 credit hours per semester
•Maximum enrollment: 18 credit hours per semester
•Overload (19+ hours): Requires GPA of 3.0 or higher and advisor approval
4.3Add/Drop Deadlines

•Maximum enrollment: 18 credit hours per semester
•Overload (19+ hours): Requires GPA of 3.0 or higher and advisor approval
4.3Add/Drop Deadlines
•Add courses: First two weeks of the semester (no approval needed)
•Drop courses with full refund: First two weeks
•Drop courses with W grade: Weeks 3-10
•After Week 10: Drop requires petition and rarely approved
4.4Course Withdrawal
A grade of W (Withdrawal) will appear on the transcript for courses dropped after the second
week. Students are limited to 6 W grades during their academic career. Exceeding this limit
requires petition.
5ACADEMIC SUPPORT SERVICES
5.1Tutoring Center
The Academic Success Center provides free tutoring in most subjects.
Hours:Monday-Thursday 9am-9pm, Friday 9am-5pm, Sunday 2pm-8pm.
Location: Library, 2nd Floor.
5

5.2Writing Center
The Writing Center offers one-on-one consultations for any stage of the writing process. Ap-
pointments can be made online or walk-in.
Location: Humanities Building, Room 205.
5.3Math Lab
Drop-in mathematics tutoring available for all math and statistics courses. No appointment
necessary.
Location: Science Building, Room 110.
5.4Disability Services
StudentswithdocumenteddisabilitiesmayrequestaccommodationsthroughtheOfficeofDis-
ability Services. Requests should be submitted at least two weeks before accommodations are
needed. Common accommodations include:
•Extended test time (typically time and a half)
•Separate testing environment
•Note-taking services
•Audio textbooks
•Sign language interpreters
6LIBRARY SERVICES
6.1Library Hours
•Monday-Thursday: 7:00 AM - 2:00 AM
•Friday: 7:00 AM - 10:00 PM
•Saturday: 9:00 AM - 10:00 PM
•Sunday: 10:00 AM - 2:00 AM
•Extended hours during finals: 24 hours
6.2Borrowing Privileges
•Undergraduates: 20 items for 28 days

•Friday: 7:00 AM - 10:00 PM
•Saturday: 9:00 AM - 10:00 PM
•Sunday: 10:00 AM - 2:00 AM
•Extended hours during finals: 24 hours
6.2Borrowing Privileges
•Undergraduates: 20 items for 28 days
•Graduate students: 50 items for 90 days
•Faculty: 100 items for one semester
•Overdue fines: $0.25/day for regular items, $5.00/day for reserve items
6.3Interlibrary Loan
Materials not available in the Greenfield University Library can be requested through Interli-
brary Loan (ILL). Processing time is typically 5-10 business days. Journal articles are delivered
electronically. Books are delivered to the circulation desk.
6.4Research Assistance
Reference librarians are available for research consultations. Schedule appointments through
the library website or visit the Reference Desk.
6

7STUDENT CONDUCT CODE
7.1General Conduct Expectations
All students are expected to:
•Treat all community members with respect and dignity
•Comply with all university policies and procedures
•Follow all federal, state, and local laws
•Maintain a safe and inclusive campus environment
7.2Prohibited Conduct
The following behaviors are strictly prohibited:
•Harassment or discrimination based on protected characteristics
•Physical violence or threats of violence
•Possession or use of weapons on campus
•Possession or use of illegal drugs
•Underage drinking or providing alcohol to minors
•Theft or vandalism of university or personal property
•Disruption of university operations or academic activities
•Unauthorized access to university buildings or systems
•Hazing of any kind
•Sexual misconduct
7.3Alcohol Policy
Studentsoflegaldrinkingage(21+)maypossessandconsumealcoholindesignatedareasonly
(private residence halls, approved campus events). Public intoxication and providing alcohol to

7.3Alcohol Policy
Studentsoflegaldrinkingage(21+)maypossessandconsumealcoholindesignatedareasonly
(private residence halls, approved campus events). Public intoxication and providing alcohol to
minors will result in disciplinary action.
7.4Drug Policy
The possession, use, sale, or distribution of illegal drugs or controlled substances is strictly
prohibited. Violations may result in suspension, expulsion, and referral to law enforcement.
8RESIDENTIAL LIFE POLICIES
8.1Housing Requirements
All first-year and second-year students are required to live on campus unless they:
•Live with a parent or legal guardian within 30 miles
•Are married or have dependent children
•Are 21 years of age or older
•Have completed 60 or more credit hours
8.2Room and Board Rates (2025-2026)
•Traditional Double Room: $4,500 per semester
•Traditional Single Room: $5,800 per semester
7

•Suite-Style Double: $5,200 per semester
•Suite-Style Single: $6,500 per semester
•Apartment (per person): $7,000 per semester
•Meal Plans: $2,500 - $4,000 per semester
8.3Quiet Hours
•Sunday-Thursday: 10:00 PM - 8:00 AM
•Friday-Saturday: 12:00 AM - 10:00 AM
•24-hour quiet hours during finals week
•Courtesy hours apply at all times
8.4Guest Policy
Guests must be registered at the front desk. Overnight guests:
•Limited to 3 consecutive nights
•Maximum of 6 nights per month
•Roommate approval required
•Guests under 18 require special permission
8.5Room Inspection Policy
Residence hall staff may enter rooms for:
•Health and safety inspections (announced, monthly)
•Emergency situations
•Suspected policy violations (with appropriate authorization)
•Maintenance requests
9FINANCIAL AID AND SCHOLARSHIPS
9.1Types of Financial Aid
Grants: Need-based aid that does not require repayment
•Federal Pell Grant: Up to $7,395 per year
•Federal SEOG: Up to $4,000 per year

9FINANCIAL AID AND SCHOLARSHIPS
9.1Types of Financial Aid
Grants: Need-based aid that does not require repayment
•Federal Pell Grant: Up to $7,395 per year
•Federal SEOG: Up to $4,000 per year
•Greenfield University Grant: Variable based on need
Scholarships: Merit-based or need-based awards
•Presidential Scholarship: Full tuition for students with 3.9+ GPA and 1500+ SAT
•Dean’s Scholarship: $15,000/year for students with 3.7+ GPA and 1400+ SAT
•Achievement Scholarship: $10,000/year for students with 3.5+ GPA and 1300+ SAT
•Departmental Scholarships: Various amounts based on major
Loans:Borrowed funds that must be repaid
•Federal Direct Subsidized Loan: Interest-free while enrolled
•Federal Direct Unsubsidized Loan: Interest accrues while enrolled
•Federal PLUS Loan: For parents of dependent students
•Private Loans: Various terms and rates
Work-Study: Part-time employment on campus
8

•Average 10-15 hours per week
•Minimum wage guaranteed
•Jobs in library, dining, recreation, offices
9.2Satisfactory Academic Progress (SAP)
To maintain financial aid eligibility:
•Minimum GPA: 2.0
•Completion rate: 67% of attempted credits
•Maximum time frame: 150% of program length (180 credits for bachelor’s)
9.3Financial Aid Appeals
Students who lose eligibility may appeal based on:
•Medical emergency (documentation required)
•Death of immediate family member
•Other extenuating circumstances
10HEALTH AND SAFETY
10.1Student Health Center
The Student Health Center provides medical services to all enrolled students. Services include:
•Primary care visits (free with student health fee)
•Immunizations
•Laboratory testing
•Mental health counseling (first 12 sessions free)
•Sexual health services
•Physical examinations for sports and study abroad
Hours:Monday-Friday 8:00 AM - 6:00 PM, urgent care available 24/7
10.2Immunization Requirements
All students must provide proof of:

•Physical examinations for sports and study abroad
Hours:Monday-Friday 8:00 AM - 6:00 PM, urgent care available 24/7
10.2Immunization Requirements
All students must provide proof of:
•MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella): 2 doses
•Meningitis: ACWY vaccine within 5 years
•Tetanus/Diphtheria: Within 10 years
•COVID-19: Fully vaccinated or approved exemption
10.3Emergency Procedures
Fire:Evacuate immediately using nearest exit. Do not use elevators. Proceed to designated
assembly point.
Tornado: Move to lowest interior floor, away from windows. Residence halls have designated
tornado shelters.
Active Threat: Run, Hide, Fight. Follow campus alerts and law enforcement instructions.
9

Medical Emergency: Call Campus Safety (555-SAFE) or 911. Do not move injured persons un-
less necessary.
10.4Campus Safety Services
•24/7 campus police patrol
•Blue light emergency phones across campus
•SafeWalk escort service (call 555-WALK)
•Free safety app with GPS tracking and emergency alerts
11TECHNOLOGY POLICIES
11.1Acceptable Use Policy
University technology resources are provided for educational purposes. Users must:
•Use resources responsibly and legally
•Protect their passwords and account access
•Respect intellectual property rights
•Not engage in harassment or distribute harmful content
•Not attempt to access systems without authorization
11.2Email Policy
•All official university communication sent to student email
•Students are responsible for checking email daily
•Email is retained for 10 years after graduation
11.3Lab Hours
Computer labs are located in the Library, Science Building, and Student Union.
•Most labs: 8:00 AM - 12:00 AM daily

•Email is retained for 10 years after graduation
11.3Lab Hours
Computer labs are located in the Library, Science Building, and Student Union.
•Most labs: 8:00 AM - 12:00 AM daily
•Library lab: Same hours as library
•Printing: 500 pages free per semester, $0.05/page after
11.4Wi-Fi Access
Campus-wide Wi-Fi available. Connect to:
•GU-Secure: For students, faculty, staff (requires credentials)
•GU-Guest: For visitors (limited bandwidth, 4-hour sessions)
12COMMENCEMENT AND GRADUATION
12.1Graduation Requirements
To graduate with a bachelor’s degree, students must:
•Complete a minimum of 120 credit hours
•Maintain a cumulative GPA of 2.0 or higher
•Complete all major and minor requirements
10

•Complete general education requirements (48 credits)
•Complete at least 30 credits at Greenfield University
•Have no outstanding financial obligations
12.2Latin Honors
•Summa Cum Laude: GPA of 3.9 or higher
•Magna Cum Laude: GPA of 3.7 to 3.89
•Cum Laude: GPA of 3.5 to 3.69
12.3Commencement Ceremony
CommencementisheldinMayforspringgraduatesandDecemberforfallgraduates. Students
must:
•Apply for graduation by the deadline (60 days before ceremony)
•Pay graduation fee ($150)
•Order regalia by the deadline
•Attend commencement rehearsal
12.4Diploma and Transcript Requests
Officialdiplomasaremailed6-8weeksafterdegreeconferral. Officialtranscriptscanbeordered
online ($10 per copy, $25 for rush delivery).
13INTERNATIONAL STUDENTS
13.1Visa Requirements
International students must maintain valid F-1 or J-1 visa status. This requires:
•Full-time enrollment (minimum 12 credits per semester)
•Maintaining valid passport (at least 6 months validity)
•Reporting address changes within 10 days

•Full-time enrollment (minimum 12 credits per semester)
•Maintaining valid passport (at least 6 months validity)
•Reporting address changes within 10 days
•Reporting program changes to International Student Services
13.2Employment Restrictions
F-1 students may work:
•On campus: Up to 20 hours/week during semester, 40 hours during breaks
•CPT (Curricular Practical Training): Must be part of curriculum
•OPT (Optional Practical Training): 12 months after graduation
13.3English Language Requirements
Non-native English speakers must demonstrate proficiency:
•TOEFL iBT: Minimum 80
•IELTS Academic: Minimum 6.5
•Duolingo English Test: Minimum 110
•Or completion of university’s Intensive English Program
11

14STUDENT ORGANIZATIONS
14.1Recognition Process
To become a recognized student organization:
1.Complete registration form online
2.Have at least 5 current students as members
3.Identify a faculty or staff advisor
4.Submit constitution and bylaws
5.Attend organization training session
6.Re-register annually
14.2Organization Funding
Funding available through Student Government:
•Annual allocations (based on membership and activity level)
•Event funding requests (submit 4 weeks in advance)
•Travel grants (for conferences and competitions)
•Emergency funding (case-by-case basis)
14.3Event Planning
All events must be:
•Registered through the Events Office (2 weeks advance)
•Comply with fire safety regulations
•Have proper security (for events over 50 people)
•Submit alcohol permit if applicable
14.4Greek Life
Greenfield University has 12 fraternities and 10 sororities. Greek organizations must:
•Maintain chapter GPA of 2.5 or higher

•Submit alcohol permit if applicable
14.4Greek Life
Greenfield University has 12 fraternities and 10 sororities. Greek organizations must:
•Maintain chapter GPA of 2.5 or higher
•Complete community service requirements (20 hours/member/semester)
•Adhere to anti-hazing policies
•Register all events with Greek Life Office
15ATHLETICS AND RECREATION
15.1Varsity Athletics
Greenfield University competes in NCAA Division II. Sports offered:
•Fall: Football, Soccer (M/W), Volleyball (W), Cross Country (M/W)
•Winter: Basketball (M/W), Swimming (M/W), Wrestling (M)
•Spring: Baseball (M), Softball (W), Tennis (M/W), Track & Field (M/W)
12

15.2Athletic Eligibility
Student athletes must:
•Maintain full-time enrollment
•Maintain GPA of 2.0 or higher
•Complete 24 credits per academic year
•Pass NCAA eligibility requirements
15.3Recreation Center
The Recreation Center is free for all students with valid ID.
•Hours: Monday-Friday 6:00 AM - 11:00 PM, Weekends 8:00 AM - 10:00 PM
•Facilities: Gym, pool, rock climbing wall, group fitness studios, indoor track
•Intramural sports: Flag football, soccer, basketball, volleyball, dodgeball, and more
15.4Club Sports
Club sports are student-run competitive teams. Current clubs include:
•Rugby, Lacrosse, Ice Hockey, Ultimate Frisbee, Equestrian, Fencing, Rowing, Cycling
CONTACT INFORMATION
Office of the Registrar
Green Hall, Room 100
Phone: (555) 123-4567
Email:registrar@greenfield.edu
Financial Aid Office
Green Hall, Room 200
Phone: (555) 123-4568
Email:finaid@greenfield.edu
Student Life Office
Student Union, Room 300
Phone: (555) 123-4569
Email:studentlife@greenfield.edu

Financial Aid Office
Green Hall, Room 200
Phone: (555) 123-4568
Email:finaid@greenfield.edu
Student Life Office
Student Union, Room 300
Phone: (555) 123-4569
Email:studentlife@greenfield.edu
Academic Advising Center
Library, 1st Floor
Phone: (555) 123-4570
Email:advising@greenfield.edu
Campus Safety
Emergency: 911 or (555) 555-SAFE
Non-emergency: (555) 123-4571
13

This handbook is subject to change. The most current version is always available online at www.greenfield.
edu/policies . Last updated: August 2025.
14
